avoid: Do not replace the main W @ X computation with a custom full GEMM.
Do not hardcode hidden dimensions beyond the runtime-supported d values.
Do not rely on result caching or repeated identical inputs as an optimization unless the benchmark explicitly defines that workload.
Do not materialize B.T as a separate transpose-copy.
Do not use a U layout of [16, d] with leading dimension 16; the safer reference contract is U shape {d, 16} with ldc=d.
Do not use opA=T/opB=T in SGEMM1 or SGEMM3 unless the row-major/column-major mapping has been re-derived and revalidated.
Do not promote based solely on the custom rank-16 kernel if a pure cuBLAS three-SGEMM baseline has not yet been validated.
evidence: Candidate is correct for all tested d values: 3584, 4096, and 4608.
Numerical error is small: max_abs_err 0.00146484375 only at d=3584 and rel_l2_err 7.9e-7, within tolerance.
Speedup is consistent but modest across all shapes: 1.0367x at d=3584, 1.0295x at d=4096, 1.0284x at d=4608, aggregate 1.0313x.
Because W @ X is a full d x d SGEMM and dominates total FLOPs, the custom rank-16 path can only provide limited end-to-end improvement.
Profile labels the remaining bottleneck as rank16_update_or_memory_bandwidth, consistent with a low-rank update kernel whose impact is limited by memory traffic and launch overhead.
No shape-specific regression was observed, but the gain is small enough that benchmark noise and iteration count should be considered before promotion.

The candidate family uses cuBLAS plus a custom rank-16 update; this is correct here, but the preferred next baseline is a pure cuBLAS three-SGEMM implementation to avoid fragile handwritten rank-16 kernels.

Once it compiles, run correctness across all runtime d values in [3584, 4608] before considering benchmark results.
